title: Do not invent Cryptol helper names or external primitives
rule: If a helper or primitive is not in the retrieved context, built-ins, or provided dependency code, do not guess its name. Either implement a tiny local helper explicitly or fall back to a conservative unresolved skeleton.
positive_strategy: Replace guessed helper calls with explicit local helpers whose types are written out.
anti_pattern: Using names like `bytesToBits`, `zeroExtend`, or `SHAKE128_Init` only because they sound plausible.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: do not invent Cryptol helper names or external primitives. When a name is unavailable, define a tiny local helper or emit a conservative unresolved skeleton.

title: Do not drive finite sequence length from a runtime value
rule: If a loop count or bound is a runtime value such as `len`, `numBlocks`, or `remaining`, do not try to turn it into a finite Cryptol sequence length. Use value-level recursion or static literal schedules instead.
positive_strategy: Choose between explicit runtime recursion and a fully static schedule before writing the loop body.
anti_pattern: `take len [0 ...]` or `[0 .. (numBlocks - 1)]` when `len`/`numBlocks` are values computed inside the function.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: runtime values must not be used as finite sequence lengths. Switch to value-level recursion or static schedules.

title: Use tuples or records for heterogeneous state
rule: If state combines arrays, counters, flags, or other fields of different types, represent it as a tuple or record. Sequences are homogeneous.
positive_strategy: Thread `(state, counter)` or `{ state = ..., counter = ... }` through recursion or `foldl`.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: heterogeneous state must use tuples or records, not sequences.

title: Do not patch a local width mismatch without tracing the whole index chain
rule: When the failing expression is an index or index arithmetic, trace width requirements back through every producer (`layer`, `len`, `start`, `j`, offsets) before changing a single signature.
positive_strategy: Design index-domain widths globally, then widen narrow fragments explicitly with a helper or `zext` at the correct boundary.
anti_pattern: Changing only one callee signature from `[8]` to `[9]` while upstream values remain narrow.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: repair index widths globally across the whole chain, not one signature at a time.

title: Generator-bound variables stay inside the comprehension
rule: A variable bound by a comprehension generator is only valid inside that comprehension expression and qualifiers. If a helper depends on it, pass it as an explicit argument or inline the calculation.
positive_strategy: Write `elem i = ...` and call it from the comprehension, or compute the dependent value inline.
anti_pattern: Referring to a generator-bound `i` from a sibling `where` binding.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: comprehension generator variables cannot leak into sibling where bindings.

title: Do not use type-level countdown to express a runtime loop
rule: If the loop/control variable is runtime logic, model it as a value-level recursion variable. Do not count down a type parameter and recursively instantiate smaller result types to mimic a runtime loop.
positive_strategy: Use a value-level counter `go j state` or choose a fixed static result shape driven by the signature.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: type-level countdown is not a substitute for runtime recursion.

title: If a parameter resolves only to a parameter family, prefer a conservative skeleton over a pseudo-concrete implementation
rule: When a symbol such as `eta` is only known to belong to a parameter family and the current function body needs a concrete semantic role, do not pretend the implementation is fully determined. Keep the code conservative and mark the unresolved dependency explicitly.
positive_strategy: Emit a minimal legal skeleton or keep the function parameterized only if the body truly remains valid under that abstraction.
anti_pattern: Producing a fully executable body that silently assumes one family member without caller evidence.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: unresolved parameter families should not be forced into pseudo-concrete executable code.

title: Comments do not satisfy dependencies
rule: A comment such as 'assume X is provided elsewhere' does not make `X` available. If a dependency is missing, either import/define it or return a conservative unresolved skeleton.
positive_strategy: Reference only names that are actually imported, defined, or provided in retrieved dependency code.
anti_pattern: Building a main implementation around commented assumptions about missing primitives.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: comments do not place dependencies in scope.

title: When a branch body looks imperative, rebuild it as an expression before fixing smaller issues
rule: If an `if` branch was translated as a statement sequence with assignment-like lines, fix the branch shape first using `where`, `let`, or a helper expression. Do not keep patching subexpressions inside an ill-formed branch.
positive_strategy: Turn multi-step branch logic into `else out where ...` or a helper function call.
anti_pattern: Continuing to edit names inside a branch that is still syntactically not a single expression.
applicable_when: repairing parse errors inside if branches
retrieval_text: Guardrail: repair branch shape first when an if branch was translated imperatively.

title: Prefer authoritative constant-table lookup over re-deriving the same value
rule: When the spec or provided context already includes an authoritative constant table, retrieve and index that table instead of inventing a secondary helper to recompute its entries.
positive_strategy: Use `table @ idx` with nearby index-normalization helpers when needed.
anti_pattern: Writing a symbolic `foo_pow idx` helper even though a trusted table is already available.
retrieval_text: Guardrail: prefer direct lookup from authoritative constant tables.
